era_context: The late reign of Shalmaneser IV of Assyria (ending 773 BCE), the early Eastern Zhou Dynasty in China during the Spring and Autumn period, and the Greek Archaic period. This year represents a period of relative stability in the Near East, with the Neo-Assyrian Empire maintaining its territorial holdings and tributary networks. Across Eurasia, iron-working technology was becoming dominant, and the foundations of classical civilizations were being established.

events.title: Shalmaneser IV of Assyria: Administrative reign and imperial stability
events.description: Shalmaneser IV continued his reign with a focus on administrative consolidation and the maintenance of Assyrian hegemony. His rule was characterized by relative military quiescence compared to his predecessors, with emphasis on collecting tribute from vassal states and maintaining the imperial bureaucracy. This period represents the mature phase of Neo-Assyrian imperial organization, with well-established systems of provincial administration and tributary relationships. The empire's territorial extent remained stable, with no major military campaigns recorded for this year.
events.certainty_note: Shalmaneser IV's reign is well-documented in Assyrian records; specific events in 780 BCE are not individually attested in surviving sources.

events.title: Eastern Zhou Dynasty: Feudal competition and technological advancement
events.description: The Eastern Zhou Dynasty continued its period of feudal fragmentation, with major states competing for regional hegemony. Iron-working technology was becoming increasingly widespread during this period, gradually replacing bronze for tools and weapons. This technological transition had significant implications for military capabilities and agricultural productivity. The period saw the development of more sophisticated irrigation systems and the expansion of agricultural land, supporting population growth and the emergence of larger, more complex political entities. The concept of the 'Mandate of Heaven' and ritual propriety (li) were becoming increasingly important in legitimizing political authority.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Eastern Zhou sites
Bronze inscriptions from Eastern Zhou states

events.certainty_note: The broad technological and political context is well-established; specific events in 780 BCE are not individually documented.

events.title: Greek Archaic period: Alphabet standardization and colonization expansion
events.description: The Greek Archaic period continued with the ongoing standardization of the Greek alphabet, which had been adapted from the Phoenician script. By this time, the alphabet was becoming increasingly standardized across Greek city-states, though regional variations persisted. Early Greek colonization was expanding throughout the Mediterranean and Black Sea regions, with settlements being established in southern Italy, Sicily, and the Levantine coast. The composition of early Greek epic poetry (Homeric epics) was likely ongoing during this era. The polis system was becoming the dominant political organization, with the development of citizen assemblies and the gradual emergence of hoplite warfare tactics.

sources.name: Later Greek historians on the Archaic period

events.certainty_note: The broad cultural context is well-established through archaeology; specific events in 780 BCE are not individually documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No major disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. Minor chronological variations exist in the dating of Shalmaneser IV's reign across different scholarly sources, but these are within ±1 year and do not significantly affect the historical narrative. The precise dating of the Greek alphabet adoption remains debated among scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 780 BCE is similar to the preceding years in this batch: well-documented for the Near East and China through administrative and epigraphic sources, but sparse for most other regions. The absence of documentation for most of the world reflects both the limited extent of literate civilizations in this period and the biases of modern scholarship toward Mediterranean and Near Eastern sources. Assyrian records are official court documents and may not reflect the full complexity of political and social life. Chinese sources are primarily later compilations, though epigraphic evidence provides some contemporary verification. Greek history relies primarily on later literary sources and archaeology. The overall documentation level for this five-year period (780–784 BCE) is moderate, with significant gaps in coverage for Africa, northern Europe, Central Asia, and the Americas.

graph_edges.note: Shalmaneser IV's reign ended in 773 BCE, succeeded by Ashur-dan III
Continuation of Eastern Zhou Dynasty following the collapse of Western Zhou in 771 BCE
